mnemonics.title: Truth Table for Connectives
mnemonics.content: Remember: AND (∧) = T only when BOTH true; OR (∨) = F only when BOTH false; IMPLIES (→) = F only when T→F; BICONDITIONAL (↔) = T when SAME; NOT (¬) just flips

mnemonics.title: Quantifier Negation Rules
mnemonics.content: Flip the quantifier AND negate the predicate: ¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x); ¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x). Think: 'Not all' = 'Some not'; 'None' = 'All not'

mnemonics.title: Valid Inference Rules
mnemonics.content:
MODUS PONENS: P→Q, P ∴ Q (Put UP, get Q)
MODUS TOLLENS: P→Q, ¬Q ∴ ¬P (Take DOWN, drop P)
HYPOTHETICAL SYLLOGISM: P→Q, Q→R ∴ P→R (Chain: P to Q to R)
DISJUNCTIVE SYLLOGISM: P∨Q, ¬P ∴ Q (Kill one, get the other)

mnemonics.title: Remembering Contrapositive
mnemonics.content:
CONTRAST: CONTRA + POSITIVE → Swap and negate
Original: p → q (if p then q)
Contrapositive: ¬q → ¬p (if NOT q then NOT p)
They are ALWAYS equivalent!

acronyms.usage: A syntactically valid logical expression in predicate logic, e.g., ∀x (P(x) → Q(x))
A formula in AND of ORs: (A ∨ B) ∧ (C ∨ D); useful for automated theorem proving
A formula in OR of ANDs: (A ∧ B) ∨ (C ∧ D)
Shows all possible truth value combinations for propositions and compound statements

visualTips.visualization: Think of IMPLIES (→) as a 'PROMISE': p → q is like 'If you do p (promise kept), I'll do q'. The promise is broken only when p happens but q doesn't (T→F = FALSE)
Draw a circle representing the DOMAIN. Quantifiers (∀, ∃) are like 'filters' - ∀ checks EVERY point inside, ∃ checks if ANY point passes the test. Variables inside the circle are BOUND.
The NOT (¬) acts like a 'bubble' that must travel past each connective, flipping AND→OR and OR→AND. Imagine ¬(p ∧ q): the bubble 'pushes' through the AND, becoming OR of negations.

Think of VALID as 'guaranteed result': If premises are true, conclusion MUST be true. It's about STRUCTURE, not truth content. A valid argument is like a perfect machine - given correct input (true premises), it produces correct output (true conclusion).

analogies.analogy: A proposition is like a specific statement ('The sky is blue') - either fully true or false. A predicate is like a question with a blank ('x is blue') - truth depends on what fills the blank (x).
∀ is like a 'universal scanner' that checks EVERY item in a set. It's like a metal detector that must beep for EVERY piece of metal to pass the test. If ONE fails, ∀ fails.
∃ is like a 'search team' that needs only ONE successful find. It's like finding ONE person who knows the secret - once found, the mission is accomplished. Finding more is fine but unnecessary.

Two logically equivalent propositions are like two different paths to the same destination. Different roads (different expressions), same result (identical truth tables). Like 'turn left then right' is equivalent to 'turn right then turn 180°'.

Bound variables are like employees within a company (quantifier) - they have a specific role defined by that company. Free variables are like freelancers - their value/meaning is independent and needs external assignment.
